title: Plan with Claude Opus, Build with Kimi K2.6? LIVE Mixed-Provider Benchmark
summary: Cole Medin benchmarks mixed-provider AI workflows by running the same coding task four ways across three real GitHub issues, varying whether Claude Opus or Kimi K2.6 handles planning versus implementation. An Opus evaluator then scores each of the 12 resulting pull requests on seven dimensions including correctness, code quality, and fidelity.
writeup: A live benchmarking session for the "dark factory," an autonomous codebase that writes and reviews its own code with no human allowed to touch it. The creator runs 12 simultaneous Archon workflows across a 2x2 matrix of model pairings (Gemini 2.6 and Claude Opus 4.8 for planning and implementation respectively) on three real GitHub issues, then scores each resulting pull request against a 7-dimension rubric. The central question is how to mix frontier models to stay under worsening rate limits without sacrificing output quality.
key_points: Archon orchestrates four named workflow variants (KK, KO, OK, OO) representing every combination of Gemini 2.6 and Opus 4.8 at the planning and implementation nodes, with Claude Sonnet used for the cheaper codebase-exploration step in all four.

The benchmark runs 12 workflows concurrently (4 model combos x 3 GitHub issues), selecting issues that stress reasoning and codebase understanding rather than raw volume of lines changed, including a cache race condition, a port mismatch, and an RRF determinism bug.

Each resulting pull request is scored on seven dimensions (issue resolution, root cause quality, scope discipline, subtle correctness, code quality, test coverage, and plan-to-implementation fidelity) for a maximum of 70 points, with smoke-test runs suggesting real results cluster around 60 out of 70.

Scope discipline emerged as a notable differentiator: Gemini 2.6 and Minimax were observed to exceed the required scope and touch unrelated code, while a top score on that dimension requires a surgical diff limited to exactly what the issue demands.

The motivation for provider mixing is explicitly economic and practical: frontier model subscriptions are increasingly rate-limited, and routing only the highest-reasoning step (planning) to the most expensive model keeps token spend proportional to task complexity.

Archon makes provider mixing a node-level configuration parameter, so switching between Claude Code for Anthropic calls and the Gemini CLI for Gemini calls requires changing only a couple of workflow parameters rather than rewriting the agent logic.
